title: Encapsulation
description: Master Java's access control system — private, protected, and public — and understand how getters, setters, and data hiding produce robust, exam-tested OOP designs that CSE110 assessors specifically target.

sections.content: 🔒 Why Encapsulation Matters in CSE110 Exams

**Encapsulation** is the OOP principle of bundling data (fields) and the methods that operate on that data into a single class, while restricting direct external access to the data. CSE110 exams test encapsulation in three ways: **access modifier questions** (what compiles, what doesn't), **getter/setter tracing** (what value is returned after a sequence of calls), and **design questions** (why should a field be `private`). Getting this chapter right is guaranteed marks.

sections.label: Exam Strategy
sections.content: On CSE110 exams, whenever you see a field declared `private` and a question asks what the output is after calling `obj.fieldName`, the answer is always a **compile error** — direct access to private fields from outside the class is illegal. Watch for this trap in every output-prediction question.

Java has four access levels. Three are explicit keywords (`private`, `protected`, `public`) and one is the default (package-private, when no keyword is used). Understanding exactly **what each level allows and forbids** is the foundation of encapsulation questions.

sections.label: Most Common Exam Mistake
sections.content: Students confuse `protected` with `private`. `protected` members ARE accessible in subclasses — even in different packages — via inheritance. `private` members are NEVER accessible outside the class, not even in subclasses. A subclass cannot access `super.privateField` directly.
🔧 Getters and Setters: The Standard Pattern

The canonical encapsulation pattern: declare fields `private`, then provide `public` **accessor** (getter) and **mutator** (setter) methods. Getters return the field value; setters validate and assign new values. This gives the class author full control over how data is read and written.

sections.title: Classic Encapsulation — BankAccount with Validation

sections.content: Inside instance methods and constructors, `this` refers to the current object. It is essential in setters when the parameter name matches the field name — without `this`, the assignment `name = name` would do nothing (assigning the parameter to itself).

sections.title: The this Keyword — Correct vs Wrong Setter

sections.label: Subtle this Bug
sections.content: If a class field and a local variable/parameter share the same name, the local variable **shadows** the field. Without `this.fieldName`, you're operating on the local variable, not the field. This produces a **logical error** — the code compiles but behaves incorrectly.
🏗️ Encapsulation with Inheritance: protected vs private

When a class is designed to be extended, choosing between `private` and `protected` for fields has important consequences. `private` fields are inherited (the subclass object has them) but are **not directly accessible** in the subclass — only through the parent's `public`/`protected` methods. `protected` fields are accessible directly in subclasses.

sections.title: private vs protected in Inheritance

sections.content: An **immutable class** has all fields `private` and `final`, no setters, and all fields set only in the constructor. Once created, the object's state cannot change. Java's `String` class is the most famous example. Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe and prevent accidental modification.

sections.title: Immutable Point Class
Exercise 1: Broken Setter Assignment
sections.error: Lines 5 and 9: `width = width` and `height = height` are logical errors — the parameter is assigned to itself, leaving the private fields at their default value of 0.0.
sections.explanation: This is a **logical error** — it compiles successfully but produces wrong results. When the parameter name is the same as the field name, `width = width` simply assigns the parameter to itself (a no-op), because the parameter name shadows the field name in that scope. The `private` field `width` is never updated and retains its default value `0.0`. The fix is to use `this.width = width` so the left-hand side explicitly refers to the instance field.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Illegal Direct Private Access
sections.error: Lines 13-14 (in TestPerson.main): `p.name` and `p.age` cause compile-time errors. Both fields are declared `private` in the `Person` class, making them inaccessible from any external class, including `TestPerson`.
sections.explanation: This is a **compile-time error**. The `private` access modifier means the field is only accessible within the class that declared it (`Person`). Any attempt to read or write `p.name` or `p.age` from `TestPerson` causes: `error: name has private access in Person`. The fix is to add `public` getter methods inside `Person` that return the field values — this is the standard encapsulation pattern.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Getter/Setter Trace with Validation
Exercise 4: protected Field Access in Subclass
sections.explanation:
1. `new Car(80, "Petrol", 4)`: calls `super(80,"Petrol")` → `speed=80`, `fuel="Petrol"` (private), then `doors=4`.
2. `c.show()`: `speed += 10` → speed becomes **90** (protected field accessed directly in subclass ✓). Prints **90**.
3. `getFuel()`: inherited public method, returns `fuel` = **Petrol**.
4. `doors` = **4**. (Note: `fuel` is private in Vehicle; `c.fuel` would be a compile error, but `getFuel()` is the public accessor used here correctly.)

sections.title: Exercise 5: Final Field Assigned Twice
sections.error: Line 7 (inside constructor): `PI = 3.14159265` is a compile-time error. `PI` is declared `final` and already initialized at the declaration site (`private final double PI = 3.14159`). A `final` field can only be assigned once — either at declaration OR in the constructor, never both.
sections.explanation: This is a **compile-time error**: `cannot assign a value to final variable PI`. A `final` instance variable must be assigned **exactly once** — either when declared (inline initialization) or in the constructor, but **not both**. The fix removes the inline `= 3.14159` initialization so the constructor assignment becomes the sole, legal assignment. Alternatively, keep the inline initialization and remove the constructor assignment.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Design an Encapsulated Student Class
sections.approach: Declare all fields private. Write a constructor that initializes all three. Getters return field values directly. setGpa validates the range [0.0, 4.0] before updating the field. toString uses string concatenation with the field names. In main, trace the two setGpa calls — one invalid (4.5 > 4.0, rejected), one valid (3.9, accepted).
sections.explanation:
**Encapsulation design decisions**:
- All fields `private` → external code cannot bypass validation.
- `id` has no setter — it is immutable after construction (identity shouldn't change).
- `setGpa` validates range before mutating → prevents invalid state (`gpa=5.0` could corrupt grade calculations).
- Constructor also validates `gpa` defensively.
**Output trace**: `toString()` after construction → `gpa=3.8`. After `setGpa(4.5)`: rejected (4.5 > 4.0), gpa stays 3.8. After `setGpa(3.9)`: accepted, gpa=3.9. Second `toString()` shows `gpa=3.9`.

items: **Encapsulation** = private fields + public getters/setters + validation in setters.
`private` is the most restrictive: only accessible within the declaring class itself — not even in subclasses.
`protected` is accessible in the same class, same package, AND subclasses (even in different packages).
Always use `this.fieldName = paramName` in setters when parameter name matches field name.
Omitting `this` when parameter shadows field is a **logical error** (compiles, wrong behavior).
A subclass **inherits** private fields but cannot access them directly — only through inherited public/protected methods.
`final` fields must be assigned **exactly once**: either at declaration OR in the constructor, never both and never neither.
**Immutable class** pattern: `private final` fields, no setters, all assignments in constructor, class declared `final`.
Access modifier violation (e.g., accessing private from outside) → **compile-time error**.
Getters that return mutable objects (like arrays) can break encapsulation — return a copy if needed.
